title: Nav
description: Nav builds side navigation and hierarchical local rails on top of tree-node behavior. It supports semantic variants, appearances, sizes, shapes, section headers, dividers, deep nesting, content templates, and quick actions.
intro: Use `ui-nav` for sidebars, workspace rails, settings navigation, and other structured navigation surfaces where a flat menu is not enough. It manages rendering and tree behavior for navigation rows, while the surrounding app handles routing or state updates when a node is selected.

featureSections.title: Basic side navigation
featureSections.description: A small flat rail is enough for simple local navigation. Use this when the section list is short and there is no need for grouping or hierarchy.

featureSections.title: Appearance, variant, and shape
featureSections.description: Appearance and variant control how prominent the navigation feels against the surrounding shell, while shape adjusts the row geometry to match your product language.

featureSections.title: Deep navigation trees
featureSections.description: Use deeper trees when a workspace contains several nested areas such as projects, releases, files, or operations. The selected path should remain understandable even across multiple levels.

featureSections.title: Sections and dividers
featureSections.description: Section headers and dividers help separate unrelated destinations inside one rail, especially in admin shells that mix navigation and utilities.

featureSections.title: Content templates with badges
featureSections.description: Custom node content is useful when navigation should carry counts, status chips, or compact metadata without building a separate sidebar component.

featureSections.title: Quick actions on nodes
featureSections.description: Quick actions fit management-style sidebars where users may pin, remove, or edit navigation items directly from the rail.

accessibility.title: Navigation semantics
accessibility.body: `ui-nav` renders its content inside a navigation region and uses tree-node semantics for hierarchical items. Section headers and dividers remain non-interactive, while actual navigation rows expose button-like interaction through the underlying tree node model.

accessibility.title: Keyboard behavior
accessibility.body:
Keyboard behavior follows the tree-node interaction model.

| Key | Action |
| --- | --- |
| Tab | Moves focus into or out of the navigation region according to document order. |
| Enter / Space | Activates the focused row according to its click behavior. |
| Arrow keys | Move between rows and expand or collapse nested items through the underlying tree navigation model. |

accessibility.title: Selected item handling
accessibility.body: Selected nodes should reflect the current location or active workspace context. When `autoScrollToSelected` is enabled, the component will attempt to bring the selected item into view after the data changes.

apiSections.title: NavComponent - ui-nav
apiSections.lead: Standalone side-navigation component built on top of tree-node rendering. It accepts navigation data, renders structural rows such as section headers and dividers, and exposes hooks for custom content and quick actions.

blocks.title: Inputs
rows: Semantic styling variant for interactive rows.
Placement of the expand or collapse chevron for items with children.
Shows or hides the selected-row indicator.
Enables a quick-actions region on each row.
Projected template for row quick actions.
Projected template for custom row content.
Attempts to scroll the selected row into view after data changes.

blocks.title: Outputs and models
rows: Navigation-specific node contract with support for structural rows.
Emits when a navigation row is activated.
Optional callback invoked when the node is activated before the component emits `nodeClick`.

paragraphs: The component does not perform routing by itself. Use `nodeClick`, selected node state, or node callbacks to integrate the rail with your router or view state.

For large navigation trees, favor clear sectioning and keep the selected path obvious. Deep hierarchies are most useful when the app genuinely has nested areas, not as a substitute for better information architecture.
blocks.title: Notes
